summary: Barros Basto’s 1928 Portuguese Haggadah offers a nearly continuous traditional Seder, unusually explicit table directions, a full Portuguese Maggid, blessings, and closing songs. It is valuable for Portuguese-reading and historical-cultural editions but needs language review, OCR verification, and English orientation for the current demo.

sourceAssessment.voice: Traditional, Portuguese, historical, domestic, explanatory, prayerful, and shaped by early-twentieth-century orthography and OCR.
bestUses: Portuguese-language or Lusophone Jewish historical supplements
Host-guide comparison of table setup and the full ritual order
Traditional long-form alternatives for Maggid, symbolic foods, blessings, and closing songs
beginnerRisks: The app currently promises English, so Portuguese passages cannot appear without an explicit language opt-in and checked English meaning.
Historical spelling and OCR errors can make even fluent readers stumble.
The source’s traditional ritual assumptions still need the project’s novice action cues.
sourceAssessment.politicalContext: This 1928 Portuguese Jewish liturgy uses biblical and traditional land, Israel, temple, and Jerusalem language. It is historical-liturgical rather than modern partisan advocacy; the Jerusalem refrain remains restricted to a traditional selection without a social-justice override.
sourceAssessment.creditContext: The edition is CC0 1.0. Credit Artur Carlos de Barros Basto and the digitization where practical, and visually verify OCR-derived Portuguese before publication.

recommendedSequences.title: Table setup and the Seder order
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Explain in English that these pages inventory the table and summarize all ritual steps; use the app’s illustrated plate guide for the actual instructions.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The two consecutive records form a compact, source-authored overview of preparation and the complete Seder sequence.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Artur Carlos de Barros Basto and the digitization where practical; identify the CC0 1.0 edition.

recommendedSequences.title: First cup through the questions
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Use clear English action cues for the wine or grape juice, parsley, salt water, and matzah, then identify the Portuguese question-and-answer text as an optional historical reading.
recommendedSequences.rationale: Four consecutive source passages carry the ritual from Kiddush through karpas and yachatz into the invitation, Four Questions, and opening answer, ending at a complete sentence boundary.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Barros Basto and identify the CC0 1.0 Portuguese edition; disclose any abridgment.

recommendedSequences.title: Teaching styles and ancestral memory
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Explain the Four Children as different ways of engaging, use inclusive modern labels outside the exact source, and then introduce the move backward into ancestral history.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The selected pages finish the obligation-to-retell teaching, complete the Four Children dialogue, and flow directly into the source’s account of the ancestors and the promise preceding enslavement.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Barros Basto and the digitization where practical; retain the source title and CC0 1.0 status.

recommendedSequences.title: Migration, oppression, cry, and deliverance
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Before reading, give the story in one English sentence: Jacob’s family migrated to Egypt, grew, was enslaved, cried out, was heard, and moved toward freedom through signs and plagues.
recommendedSequences.rationale: Seven consecutive pages provide a complete causal Exodus arc rather than a list of disconnected names or symbols.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Artur Carlos de Barros Basto; identify the CC0 1.0 historical edition.

recommendedSequences.title: Dayenu and the three symbols
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Explain Dayenu as cumulative gratitude, then point to the shank bone or substitute, matzah, and bitter herb as the source explains each symbol.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The sequence preserves the end of Dayenu, its summation, and the complete turn into the central Passover, matzah, maror, and every-generation teachings.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Barros Basto and identify the CC0 1.0 source; preserve the source order if excerpted.

recommendedSequences.title: Second cup, ritual foods, and grace
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Give the practical English action first for washing, eating matzah and maror, making the sandwich, dining, and beginning grace; the Portuguese remains an optional liturgical witness.
recommendedSequences.rationale: These source-order pages preserve the table’s full transition from the second cup through symbolic eating and the meal into communal grace.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Artur Carlos de Barros Basto and the digitization; identify the CC0 1.0 source.

recommendedSequences.title: Portuguese cumulative closing songs
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Tell guests that these are cumulative end-of-Seder songs, provide a simple refrain and an English synopsis, and make Portuguese participation optional.
recommendedSequences.rationale: Four consecutive end pages preserve the rising count of Echad Mi Yodea and the complete narrative chain of Chad Gadya.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Artur Carlos de Barros Basto and identify the CC0 1.0 historical source.

reasons: OCR-damaged title and publishing material belongs in source metadata, not the reading stream.
Canonical page has no searchable text and is quarantined.

notes: The source is retained as a substantial Portuguese and historical contribution, not excluded because the current app language is English.
The Jerusalem refrain outside these reviewed sequences may be considered only under the traditional-without-social-justice rule.
All OCR-derived text requires visual and Portuguese-language review before runtime approval; these sequences remain candidates only.
